ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for 'less sugar cereal drink'; closest match is the general cereal beverage class. The 'less sugar' qualifier is a product formulation variant not separately coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers cereal-based beverages broadly; no specific sub-code exists for reduced-sugar instant cereal drink variants.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for powdered cereal drink (less sugar) as a distinct food entity.

serving.description: 1 prepared cup (approximately 200 ml, made from ~25 g powder)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 ml of prepared cereal drink, made by dissolving about 25 g of powder in hot water, yielding roughly 55 kcal per 100 g of prepared beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Cereal drink (less sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Cereal drink (less sugar)', record ID D04010002. All per-100g values are as reported. This is a canonical Singapore HPB record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of cereal-based beverages — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for instant cereal drinks and grain-based beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for 'Cereal drink (less sugar)' was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. A GI of 65 (medium) is estimated based on analogous instant cereal grain beverages in the database, which typically range from 55–70. The glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 8.67 × 0.25 kg serving fraction) / 100 ≈ 5.6 for a 200 ml prepared serving containing approximately 8.67 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The input record's embedded GI value of 65 with confidence 0.3 is consistent with this analogous estimate and has been retained with the same medium classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content despite low sodium; insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is low at 20 mg per 100 g, this product contains added sugar which promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation; patients with hypertension should choose genuinely unsweetened cereal drinks or plain grain beverages with zero added sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar and medium glycemic index (GI 65); classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage variant
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite the 'less sugar' label, this product still contains added sugar and has a medium GI of approximately 65, making it unsuitable for patients with type 2 diabetes; zero-sugar or unsweetened grain beverages should be substituted.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar intake linked to elevated triglycerides and adverse lipid profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of added-sugar beverages, even in reduced-sugar formulations, is associated with elevated triglycerides and unfavourable lipid profiles; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit intake and prefer unsweetened whole-grain beverages.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This product contains added sugar and lacks omega-3 fatty acids and significant polyphenols; patients with rheumatoid arthritis should replace it with unsweetened herbal teas or plain oat milk to avoid pro-inflammatory dietary patterns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cereal Drink (Less Sugar) is a powdered instant beverage made from cereal grains, formulated with a reduced quantity of sugar compared to the standard variant. It is prepared by dissolving the powder in hot water and is commonly consumed as a breakfast or morning drink in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. The texture is smooth and slightly viscous when prepared, with a mild, lightly sweet grain flavour. Per 100 g of prepared beverage, it provides 55 kcal, 1.26 g protein, 1.0 g fat, 8.67 g carbohydrate, 2.54 g sugar, 1.58 g dietary fibre, 20 mg sodium, 43 mg calcium, and 0.22 mg iron. Despite the 'less sugar' designation, this product still contains added sugar and is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage variant for clinical dietary assessment purposes. It is not recommended for patients with type 2 diabetes or those managing hypertension, as added sugar promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and glycaemic excursions. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 65 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 5.6 per standard 200 ml serving. The product is ultra-processed, typically fortified with calcium and iron, and may contain milk solids or palm-based creamers. It is widely available in Singapore supermarkets and provision shops under various brand names.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record D04010002), which is the primary canonical source for Singapore food composition data. All core macros and secondary nutrients are as reported by HPB with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate consistent with the embedded value; confidence for GI is medium.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this product as of the access date. Detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel beyond calcium and iron, and moisture data are not available in the HPB source for this record. GI value of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable instant cereal grain beverages; the original input embedded GI of 65 with confidence 0.3 is consistent with this estimate and has been retained. This food is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage variant for clinical purposes despite the 'less sugar' label, as it still contains added sugar during manufacture. The dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input cultural section are noted but not carried forward as clinical endorsements given the added sugar content. Multilingual local names are now included as this is a product widely consumed in Singapore, making the G6 requirement applicable.
